title: Yellowing on White Cotton
stainChemistry: Yellowing on white cotton has multiple causes: oxidized body oils (most common on white clothing), degradation of optical brighteners in detergent, cellulose oxidation from age/storage, and residual product buildup. The yellow color comes from chromophore formation in oxidized organic compounds. Heat and time accelerate the process. Chlorine bleach can cause permanent yellowing on synthetics.
whyThisWorks: An oxygen-based bleach (OxiClean/sodium percarbonate) oxidizes the yellow chromophores back to colorless on white cotton. For body oil yellowing, a degreasing pre-treatment addresses the oil that oxidized in the first place. Sunlight provides additional natural bleaching through UV oxidation. For whites, a borax soak can help restore brightness by boosting the detergent's cleaning power.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Remove loose material mechanically — brush, scrape, or vacuum as appropriate
2. Apply NSD to the stain area — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Apply appropriate secondary agent based on stain response — 5-10 min dwell time
4. Flush with cool water. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

steps: 1. Dissolve 1-2 scoops OxiClean in hottest water safe for the fabric. Soak — 1-6 hours
2. Check progress every hour — some yellowing requires extended soak time
3. Wash in appropriate temperature water with regular detergent
4. Air dry in direct sunlight if possible — UV provides additional whitening
5. Repeat cycle if needed — stubborn yellowing may require 2-3 treatments
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: Avoid prolonged exposure to concentrated acid
fiberSensitivities: Most chemically resistant textile — tolerates aggressive treatment
Can handle chlorine bleach, hydrogen peroxide, oxygen bleach

escalation.when: If yellowing stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is yellowing on white cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
